perturbation.original_text: The Parties acknowledge that Anaheim is a public entity subject to the California Public Records Act, and the Ralph M. Brown Act; except to the extent permitted by law, the Parties may, without violating this Agreement, disclose matters that are made confidential by this Agreement to governmental officials or the public as required by any law, regulation, order, rule, ruling or other requirement of law (“Law”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, if any Recipient receives a request for Confidential Information, Recipient shall give Disclosing Party prompt written notice at the address designated herein prior to any disclosure in sufficient time to allow Disclosing Party, at its sole cost and expense, to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy concerning such disclosure. Recipient will disclose only such information as it determines is legally required and will use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatme nt for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, but it will cooperated in any efforts to do so by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that Anaheim is a public entity subject to the California Public Records Act, and the Ralph M. Brown Act; except to the extent permitted by law, the Parties may, without violating this Agreement, disclose matters that are made confidential by this Agreement to governmental officials or the public as required by any law, regulation, order, rule, ruling or other requirement of law (“Law”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, if any Recipient receives a request for Confidential Information, Recipient shall give Disclosing Party prompt written notice at the address designated herein prior to any disclosure. Recipient will disclose such information as it determines is legally required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in sufficient time to allow Disclosing Party, at its sole cost and expense, to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy concerning such disclosure' eliminates a crucial safeguard for confidential information, and removing the phrase 'will use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, but it will cooperated in any efforts to do so by Disclosing Party' severely weakens the obligation of the recipient to protect confidential information when disclosure is legally required. This could violate California Public Records Act exemptions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Public Records Act (Cal. Gov. Code §§ 6250-6276.48)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially forcing disclosure under the California Public Records Act without adequate opportunity for the disclosing party to seek legal remedies or ensure confidential treatment where legally permissible. Cal. Gov. Code § 6254 provides exemptions to public records requests, and the original text ensured the Disclosing Party had time to utilize these exemptions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, GOV 6254 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that the recipient of confidential information would give the disclosing party 'sufficient time to allow Disclosing Party, at its sole cost and expense, to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy concerning such disclosure' before any disclosure. It also explicitly stated that 'Recipient will use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatme nt for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, but it will cooperated in any efforts to do so by Disclosing Party.' These clauses were removed. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation confirm this change weakens the protection of confidential information by removing the Disclosing Party's ability to seek legal remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions California Code, GOV 6254, which the Law Explanation expands on by saying it 'provides exemptions to public records requests, and the original text ensured the Disclosing Party had time to utilize these exemptions.' Because the omission removes the Disclosing Party's guaranteed opportunity to respond before disclosure and also weakens the obligation of the recipient to protect the confidential information, a legal contradiction is created, as this directly affects the Disclosing Party's ability to leverage exemptions.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will notify each emp loyee, agent, affiliate , representative, or consultant involved in the business discussions or who may have any occasion to view, handle, or obtain any of the Confidential Information, of the terms of this Agreement and shall require such person to enter i nto an agreement with terms similar to this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will notify each employee, agent, affiliate, representative, or consultant involved in the business discussions or who may have any occasion to view, handle, or obtain any of the Confidential Information, of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for these individuals to enter into agreements with similar terms, the contract weakens the protection of confidential information. This omission undermines the reasonable steps required to maintain confidentiality under California law related to trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: This change decreases the measures taken to protect trade secrets. Under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, reasonable efforts must be made to maintain secrecy. Not requiring employees/agents to sign similar agreements makes it more difficult to prove reasonable efforts were taken, thereby contradicting the spirit and intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The omission of the requirement for employees, agents, etc., to enter into agreements with similar terms weakens the protection of confidential information. The changed text removes a measure that would help demonstrate reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' which can include 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By not requiring these individuals to sign agreements, the contract creates a higher risk of such breaches and makes it harder to prove that reasonable steps were taken to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, thus contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets under California law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, dissemination, or publication of the Confidential Information as Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using a minimal degree of care to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, dissemination, or publication of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'a minimal degree of care' significantly reduces the standard of care required to protect confidential information. This could be considered a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and therefore a violation of California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the standard of care to 'minimal' is likely insufficient to meet the requirement of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, contradicting California Civil Code § 3426.1. Such a low standard might be deemed negligence, increasing the risk of misappropriation.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text requires a 'reasonable degree of care' to protect confidential information, while the changed text reduces this to a 'minimal degree of care.' This contradicts the principle outlined in California Civil Code § 3426.1, as interpreted by the Law Explanation, which states that 'reasonable efforts' are needed to maintain trade secret secrecy. The scraped snippet from CIV 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation,' implying a legal duty to prevent unauthorized disclosure. Reducing the standard of care to 'minimal' could be seen as failing to uphold this duty, thus creating a legal contradiction by increasing the risk of misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will return to the Disclosing Party , upon demand or in the event either Party ceases to be interested in pursuing the Purpose, all Confidential Information provided to Recipient, including all copies thereof which may have been made by or on behalf of Recipient, and Recipient shall destroy, or cause to be destroyed, all notes or memoranda or other stored information of any kind prepared by Recipient relating to the Confi dential Information or discussions generally.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will return to the Disclosing Party , upon demand all Confidential Information provided to Recipient, including all copies thereof which may have been made by or on behalf of Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause requiring the recipient to destroy 'all notes or memoranda or other stored information of any kind prepared by Recipient relating to the Confidential Information or discussions generally,' the contract allows the recipient to retain potentially sensitive derivative materials even after the relationship ends. This weakens the protection of trade secrets and confidential information, potentially violating California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: Failure to destroy derivative materials increases the risk of misappropriation. While the return of original documents is required, the retention of notes/memoranda weakens trade secret protection by potentially allowing the Recipient to reverse engineer the information. This undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement under California Civil Code § 3426.1.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the recipient to destroy notes, memoranda, or other stored information related to the confidential information. This omission potentially weakens the protection of trade secrets, as the recipient could retain derivative materials. According to the Law Explanation, retaining such materials increases the risk of misappropriation and undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement for trade secret protection under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' under California Civil Code § 3426.1, which implicitly requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By not mandating the destruction of derivative materials, the modified contract fails to ensure these reasonable efforts, creating a legal contradiction as it weakens trade secret protection.
